coredata.dc:title: Luminescent hybrid biocomposite films derived from animal skin waste
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Nishimura, Shohei
Narayan, Neel
Sahin, Onur
McWilliams, Ashleigh D. Smith
Miller, Kristen A.
Salpekar, Devashish
Wang, Zixing
Joyner, Jarin
Martí, Angel A.
Vajtai, Robert
Ashokkumar, Meiyazhagan
Ajayan, Pulickel M.
coredata.dc:description: The leather industry generates approximately ten million tons of solid bio-wastes that can be used to synthesize various multifunctional materials with exciting properties. One such approach involves developing advanced hybrid materials, which is considered one of the key areas but the underdeveloped materials science discipline. This work explored methods to use collagen derived from leather industry bio-wastes to form hybrid films via two different ways: to produce luminescent carbon dots (Cdots) and develop hybrid films. The polymeric collagen was mixed with Cdots and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to create flexible composite films that exhibit improved thermal, mechanical, electrical, and luminescent properties. The Cdot films displayed enhanced luminescence. The Kubelka-Munk transformation of the diffuse reflectance reveals a massive increase in absorbance in the visible light region with the addition of rGO. Besides, the developed films displayed electrical conductivity and weak ferromagnetic characteristics and showed enhanced biocompatibility. These findings highlight new avenues for converting industrial bio-wastes into useful multifunctional materials in scalable and inexpensive ways, thereby diminishing environmental pollution and enhancing environmental sustainability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Hybrid composites
Graphene oxide
Biowastes
Tanneries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic showing the preparation of the hybrid luminescent films from animal skin waste; (b) luminescent films (inset) flexible rGO incorporated film; (c) Raman spectrum and (d) XRD analysis of the derived rGO.

shows (a) lower (b) higher magnification TEM results (inset) displays the d spacing and (c) SEM image of the derived rGO (d), and the inset shows a high-resolution TEM image of Cdots (e) particle size analysis as seen through AFM and (f) DLS technique.

(a) FTIR analysis of the collagen, Cdot, rGO, and the developed hybrid film (100/0/2 wt.% Col/Cdot/rGO) and (b) TGA analysis of the collagen, Cdot, rGO, and the developed hybrid film (inset show TGA graphs of synthesized rGO powder). (c) VSM analysis of the select 100/0/2 wt.% Col/Cdot/rGO film displaying weak ferromagnetic characteristics (inset) shows a magnified view of the hysteresis loop (d) electrical conductivity measurement of the formed hybrid films as a function of an increase in rGO proportion.

shows the digital images of the derived hybrid films (a) shows the transparency and non-luminescence of the film without rGO under normal daylight conditions; (b, c) show the films with higher proportions of rGO addition (i.e., 100/0/2 wt.% Col/Cdot/rGO) which are flexible and non-transparent; (d, e) display the luminescence behavior of the Cdot incorporated film (i.e., 100/2/0 wt.% Col/Cdot/rGO) under the UV lamp, and (f, g) show the stretchability and flexibility of the developed luminescent hybrid film.

(a) Kubelka-Munk Transformation of the diffuse reflectance, and (b) emission. (c) Biocompatibility studies of the film components; collagen, rGO, and Cdots. Shows statistical significance at p < 0.05.
Mechanical property of collagen and the developed hybrid films.

head.ce:title: Luminescent hybrid biocomposite films derived from animal skin waste

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & NanoEngineering, Rice University, Houston, TX 77005, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Materials Science & NanoEngineering, Rice University, Houston, TX 77005, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rice University, Houston, TX 77005, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Rice University, Houston, TX 77005, United States

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The leather industry generates approximately ten million tons of solid bio-wastes that can be used to synthesize various multifunctional materials with exciting properties. One such approach involves developing advanced hybrid materials, which is considered one of the key areas but the underdeveloped materials science discipline. This work explored methods to use collagen derived from leather industry bio-wastes to form hybrid films via two different ways: to produce luminescent carbon dots (Cdots) and develop hybrid films. The polymeric collagen was mixed with Cdots and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to create flexible composite films that exhibit improved thermal, mechanical, electrical, and luminescent properties. The Cdot films displayed enhanced luminescence. The Kubelka-Munk transformation of the diffuse reflectance reveals a massive increase in absorbance in the visible light region with the addition of rGO. Besides, the developed films displayed electrical conductivity and weak ferromagnetic characteristics and showed enhanced biocompatibility. These findings highlight new avenues for converting industrial bio-wastes into useful multifunctional materials in scalable and inexpensive ways, thereby diminishing environmental pollution and enhancing environmental sustainability.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of biodegradable, multifunctional composites with improved thermal [1], electrical [2,3], and physical properties, and biodegradability [4] has gained significant attention due to their potential applications in various fields of materials science. In general, hybrid composites are developed through the homogeneous blending of different inherent components to perform a multitude of functions [4]. Numerous reports are available for the development of hybrid polymer composites for a broad range of applications, such as membranes [5], water purification [6], artificial skin [7], etc. Among them, the development of engineered composites using biomass-derived polymers with improved characteristics and nanoscale features is considered advantageous due to concerns regarding environmental protection [8] and their potential applications in various fields of materials science and nanotechnology [9–11].

In a broader context, biodegradable polymers are usually derived from materials that occur in nature during the growth cycle of organisms [12], as well as other biomass sources, such as microorganisms [13], plants [14], and animals [15]. More importantly, biomass-derived polymers are renewable, sustainable [16], non-toxic, and decomposable [17]. Collagen can be considered one of the best polymers and is the most prevalent protein in animal bodies [18]. Therefore, it is used as a precursor in a variety of applications. It possesses many unique properties and is biodegradable [19], which helps reduce stress on the environment. Collagen can also be extracted from various animal sources [20], making the resource renewable, easily accessible, and cost-effective [21]. In the past, several studies have suggested the formation of collagen-based materials for numerous biological applications such as cosmetics, drug delivery [22], wound dressing [23], etc. Nevertheless, studies on the formation of multifunctional composites, such as films and sponges/scaffolds using collagen as a precursor, are limited. Moreover, without additional components, pristine collagen is not suitable for most applications [24]. However, blending collagen with fillers and other elements makes them an exciting candidate for many applications [25]. For example, we recently reported methods to develop flexible films and light-weight sponges for sensor and oil-adsorption applications [26]. Similarly, the homogeneous blending of collagen with fillers, such as rGO, graphene, Cdot, etc., helps in the formation of luminescent [27], conducting polymer composites [28].

On the other hand, discovering a new class of carbon nanomaterials, Cdots / carbon nanodots, has gained significant attention because of its size-dependent fluorescence properties, with essential applications in materials science and nanotechnology [29]. To date, several techniques have been explored for the synthesis of Cdots, but still, scaling-up and toxicity issues are considered significant roadblocks in the commercialization of this material. Hence, utilization of biomass-derived collagen, a rich source of amino acid, provides a higher percentage of carbon content and can be considered as a biocompatible, economical, and efficient alternative for Cdot synthesis compared to various carbon-based precursors. The Cdots are well-known for its size dependant properties and recently studied as an emerging candidate for replacing metal-based semiconductors and several other applications. Likewise, rGO, which is usually considered to be chemically derived graphene (also known as functionalized graphene), is reported to possess significant conductivity and porosity [30] and, hence, used in many different fields such as batteries [31], supercapacitors [32], and biomedical applications [33]. Notably, various functionalities such as carboxyl help in the efficient bonding of rGO with the polymer matrix, which increases the mechanical properties of composites inherently. Additionally, rGO has been shown to exhibit excellent conductivity, biocompatibility, and cellular uptake. Therefore, rGO is considered a promising material that can offer superior mechanical, chemical, and biological properties. Overall, some of the unique characteristics discussed above exhibited by both Cdots and rGO makes them an exciting material for several applications related to luminescence, biomedicine, materials science, etc. [34–36].

We report methods to develop hybrid composite films by blending rGO and Cdots in various weight proportions within the collagen matrix in our current work. The addition of Cdots leads to luminescence characteristics [37], and the incorporation of rGO helps enhance electrical conductivity [38] of the formed hybrid films. The integration of luminescence and electrical conductivity into the biodegradable hybrid film is expected to bring a paradigm shift in collagen-based applications.

Fig. 1 a shows the schematic of the hybrid composites' formation using Cdot, rGO, and collagen as precursors. In brief, Cdot and rGO were mixed with the polymeric collagen in different weight percentages (wt.%) to derive the hybrid films (Fig. 1b). The exact method of the development of hybrid composite films is described in the experimental section. Raman analysis of the synthesized rGO shows two intense peaks at 1360 and 1598 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G band, respectively (Fig. 1c). The D and G bands (ID/IG) are used to determine graphene materials' defects. The synthesized rGO displayed an ID/IG ratio of ∼0.89, indicating the presence of a considerable amount of sp [2] carbon networks. The 2D band positioned at 2881 cm−1 indicates the presence of graphene layers in the synthesized rGO. Fig. 1d shows the XRD analysis of the rGO displaying a broad (002) diffraction peak at
2 θ =
24 ∘
and a weak peak
∼ 2 θ =
42 ∘
, indicating the successful conversion of graphite to stacked rGO layers.

High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) analysis of the derived rGO and Cdot are shown in Fig. 2 . HRTEM images of rGO demonstrated in Fig. 2a, and b exhibits crystallinity and a high degree of exfoliation due to the oxidation process. The rGO sheets displayed wrinkled morphology with multiple layers of semi-transparent, ultra-thin sheets stacked over each other. Well resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.40 Å (Fig. 2b inset) closely matches d002 diffraction planes of functionalized graphene oxide. The slight increase in the d value might be due to the presence of several functional groups in the graphene oxide. Similarly, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis of the synthesized rGO shows free-standing sheets with significant porosities, indicating the successful conversion of GO flakes to rGO thin sheets (Fig. 2c). This observation was well supported by Raman, which showed the presence of a few layers. On the other hand, the HRTEM analysis of the derived Cdots (Fig. 2d and inset) shows several crystalline Cdots particles with a particle size of ∼2 nm (Fig. 2d). The particle size of the derived Cdots was measured using Atomic force microscopy (Fig. 2e) and Zetasizer (Fig. 2f), which displays a maximum number of particles between 1 and 3 nm. This observation coincides with the TEM results.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra obtained from pure collagen, Cdots, rGO, and a hybrid composite film are shown in Fig. 3 a. The pristine collagen displays characteristic bands at 3334, 2888, 1635, 1530, 1447, and 1240 cm−1. The band at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching coupled with NH bending vibrations of amide I. The amide II band observed at 1530 cm−1 resembles NH bending vibrations associated with CN stretching vibrations. The amide III bands are usually weak and are found in the range of 1240 cm−1, consisting of components from CN stretching and NH in-plane bending to form amide linkages. The intense band arising at 3331 cm−1 is due to vibrations of hydroxyl (OH) groups, and the weak band at 1447 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of COH groups. The FTIR spectrum of Cdots resembles collagen; however, the sharp peak observed ∼2900 cm−1 corresponding to CH2 stretching vibrations seems more prevalent than collagen. The rGO shows two major peaks at around 1410 and 1700 cm−1, corresponding to the carboxyl (COO-) group and C=O stretching, respectively, and two minor peaks around 2900 and 2850 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric CH stretching of –CH3 and symmetric vibrations of –CH2 groups, respectively. It is interesting to note that the composite hybrid film exhibits peaks corresponding to collagen, Cdots (2900 and 1530 cm−1), and rGO (1500 and 1250 cm−1), indicating the homogenous mixing of the polymer with the fillers.

We further tested the thermal stability of the collagen, Cdots, rGO and developed hybrid films using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The pristine collagen film and the Cdots displayed decomposition temperatures (Td) of 322 and 323 °C, respectively (Fig. 3b). However, the pristine rGO shows two significant weight losses; the peak observed ∼205 °C might be due to the degradation of the loosely bonded functional groups, while the latter peak, at ∼365 °C, is due to the complete loss of all the functionalities present on the derived rGO. Interestingly, all the developed hybrid film was found to display an appreciable increase in the decomposition temperature ranging between 336 and 358 °C due to the addition of rGO. The addition of a minimal amount of rGO was found to display a significant increase in the Td of the formed hybrid films.

Vibrating Sample Magnetometry (VSM) measurement of the hybrid film with a higher proportion of rGO loading (i.e., 100/0/2 wt.% Col/Cdot/rGO film) displays weak ferromagnetic properties with a saturation magnetization of ∼0.07 emu/g (Fig. 3c). The addition of rGO to the hybrid film induces magnetic properties, displaying weak ferromagnetic behavior, which marks this material as a possible alternative for sensors and actuator applications. Pristine collagen is reported to show poor electrical conductivity [39]. However, incorporating rGO into the collagen matrix increases electrical conductivity significantly (Fig. 3d). This increase in conductivity could be due to three main reasons: (i) the presence of ionic charges arising in collagen, and Cdots helps form complexes with the oppositely charged ions that assist in charge-transfer between complexes. This process causes a redistribution of ionic carriers and enhances mobile ions and electronic carrier transport across the interface providing electrical stimulus, which results in a change in the conductivity. (ii) the existence of sp [2] and sp [3] hybridized hexagonal carbon lattices in rGO, generate delocalized π electron transport which donates additional electrons to the π electron system, and (iii) the presence of electron-rich hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups, promotes the π- π electron delocalization and correspondingly increases the electrical conductivity.

The mechanical property of pristine collagen and hybrid films were analyzed, and their results as a function of the proportion of Cdot and rGO are shown in Table 1 . It is seen that the tensile property of the hybrid film decreases gradually with the addition of rGO. The tensile strength at break value is reduced from 8.1 to 5.8 N with an increase in rGO concentration. The addition of a higher amount of rGO leads to easy fracture of the sample where a higher percentage of the break was observed for 100/0/2 wt% Col/Cdot/rGO samples. This behavior might be possibly due to the rGO particle's aggregation in the polymer matrix, which resulted in weak collagen-rGO reinforcement.

On the other hand, the Cdot rich films displayed improved stress-strain behavior. When the concentration of Cdot increased from 0 to 2 wt.%, the tensile stress value rises from 19.0 to 25.8 Mpa, and the tensile strength at break value increased from 6.8 to 8.1 N. The high mechanical property of the Cdot rich films might be due to the homogenous blending of Cdot with the collagen. Besides, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in Cdots assist in efficient binding with amines present in the collagen matrix, thereby providing excellent mechanical property. The observation infers that the Cdots acts as a filler providing additional elastic support and improved mechanical property suggesting more elastic behavior. More importantly, all the composite films retained it's the flexibility to a certain extend and exhibited a better mechanical property than the pristine collagen (100/0/0 wt% Col/Cdot/rGO) films.

We further studied the changes in transparency and luminescence of the developed hybrid films due to the amount of Cdots and rGO added to the collagen films (Fig. 4 ). The Cdot incorporated hybrid films were transparent and non-luminescent under ordinary daylight (Fig. 4a). However, the films displayed bright luminescent characteristics under the UV lamp's illumination (Fig. 4d–g). On the other hand, the hybrid films' transparency was reduced significantly as the proportion of rGO increased (Fig. 4b and c). Most importantly, all of the derived hybrid films displayed excellent flexibility and stretchability (Fig. 4c, f, and g). Hence, we envision that our developed conducting, flexible, magnetic, and bio-based films can be used for a wide array of applications, including stimulation of tissue growth, environmental sensors, guided drug delivery, etc.

To quantify these observations, we analyzed the films spectroscopically by diffuse reflectance and fluorescence measurements (Fig. 5 a and b). The Kubelka-Munk transformation of the diffuse reflectance reveals a massive increase in absorbance in the visible light region with the addition of rGO. This observation correlated with the reduced transparency when rGO was added. The films without rGO display a peak absorbance at around 280 nm, which has been previously attributed to aromatic amino acids (tyrosine and phenylalanine) found in collagen [40]. Additionally, all films produce an emission peak around 300 nm due to fluorescence from the same amino acid residues. This emission is significantly quenched in the films containing rGO, likely due to the rGO absorption of emitted light. On the contrary, the films containing Cdots show an enhancement in the emission, as was visually observed in Fig. 4.

Finally, we studied the cytocompatibility of the derived hybrid films (Fig. 5c). The presence of rGO helps in significantly improving electrical conductivity. It has been reported that the presence of electrical conductivity helps in enhancing the stimulation of tissues and nerves and assisting in cellular growth and adhesion. The materials' biocompatibility was studied using human Panc-1 pancreatic cancer cells cultured with 0.01 mg/ml of either collagen, Cdots, or rGO for one day and three days. As seen in Fig. 5c, the cells cultured with the materials for one day showed high biocompatibility with nonsignificant variability between the groups (p > 0.05). While all materials exhibited high cellular compatibility when cultured for three days, cells cultured in the rGO and Cdot groups exhibited significant (p < 0.05, p < 0.01 respectively) growth, suggesting the exceptional biocompatibility of these materials.

In brief, we explored a simple method to develop hybrid composite films by mixing rGO in varying proportions with polymer and Cdots derived from leather-industry wastes. The addition of Cdots was found to induce luminescence, demonstrating an enhanced emission compared to pure collagen or rGO films. On the other hand, the incorporation of rGO reduced transparency and luminescence but significantly improved the electrical conductivity and thermal stability and displayed a weak ferromagnetic behavior. Similarly, the Kubelka-Munk transformation of the diffuse reflectance showed a massive increase in absorbance in the visible light region due to the addition of rGO. More importantly, the developed materials exhibited high cellular compatibility indicating no toxic effects. Overall, our approach explores the possibility of converting industrial bio-wastes into useful multifunctional materials with extensive applications in several areas of materials science and biomedicine besides minimalizing environmental pollution and enhancing sustainability.

The animal skin wastes disposed of in slaughterhouses were collected, processed, and purified using conventional techniques to prepare the hide powder. In brief, the skin wastes were fleshed, dehaired, delimed, and soaked in acetone/methanol (40/60 vol%) for the complete removal of the absorbed moisture [41]. After processing, the skin pieces were vacuum dried and ground in a Wiley mill to form a fine hide powder of mesh size 2 mm. The derived hide powders were used for the preparation of composite films.

About 3 L of collagen (20 mg/mL) stock solution was prepared by soaking the hide powder in 0.5 N acetic acid followed by blending it sporadically to form a viscous collagen solution. The derived solution was used as a polymer matrix to create the hybrid composites.

A few grams of hide powder was soaked in deionized water (DI) and stirred gently to swell the fibers completely. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon liner and sealed tightly in a stainless-steel autoclave. It was then heated at 180 °C for 4 h [29]. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature and filtered through a Millipore (0.025 μm) membrane. The filtered Cdots were stored in a container and used as fillers to form the hybrid films.

The rGO is prepared from graphite flakes using the Modified Hummer's Method. The graphite flakes were added into sulfuric acid, and then the mixture was sonicated for 2 h to produce a fine dispersion. This was followed by continuous stirring for 6 h at 80 °C. The formed pre-oxidized graphite was further mixed with sulfuric acid and potassium permanganate with constant stirring while maintaining the mixture below 10 °C for 2 h. The formed thick slurry was diluted with distilled water, mixed with hydrogen peroxide, and left undisturbed for 24 h for precipitation. The upper supernatant was centrifuged, and the collected residue was washed with a mixture of hydrochloric acid and distilled water repeatedly. The residue was then dried at 100 °C in a vacuum oven. The formed GO was taken in a round bottom flask, mixed with water, and then sonicated to produce a homogeneous solution. Hydrazine hydrate was added dropwise, and the solution was heated in a water bath at 80 °C for 24 h. The precipitate was separated by filtration and washed with distilled water to form rGO powder.

The hybrid biocomposite films were prepared by blending the rGO and Cdots with collagen. The collagen concentration was fixed at 20 mg/mL, and the weight percent (wt.%) of rGO and Cdots were added in different proportions such as 0.5, 1, and 2 wt.% stirred at 30 ± 5 °C for 12 h to form a homogenous blend. The formed mixtures were poured into Teflon plates and dried at room temperature to obtain hybrid (Col/Cdot/rGO) biocomposite films. The formed hybrid films were found to have a thickness of 65 ± 10 μm.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the synthesized rGO were analyzed using a Rigaku D/Max Ultima II diffractometer with CuKα radiation. The Raman spectra of rGO were obtained using the Renishaw InVia laser Raman microscope using a 50x objective lens, 632.8 nm (He–Ne) laser beam, and at 1800 lines/mm grating. High-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) analysis of the synthesized rGO was carried out using a 400F ESEM FEG operated at 20 kV with a 5 nm gold coating. Room temperature magnetic measurements of the select biocomposite film (100/0/2 wt.% of Col/Cdot/rGO) were obtained using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The HRTEM analysis was carried out at 80 kV using FEI Titan Themis [3] S/TEM equipped with a double Cs-corrector and an electron monochromator. The Cdots were diluted, dropped onto a holey carbon grid, and dried overnight in a desiccator before analysis. AFM measurements were carried out using nanoscope analysis software to analyze the particle size. The Cdots were dispersed in water and dried in a vacuum desiccator before analysis. Zetasizer Nano: Malvern Zen 3600 Zetasizer is used to study the distribution of Cdots. The Cdots were dispersed in water before measurements. The samples were scanned three times, and the cumulative readings were plotted. Mechanical property characterizations were carried out using the Instron universal testing machine to understand the developed films' stress-strain behavior. The samples were cut into a dumb-bell shape and tested using a 500 N load cell at a 5 mm/min crosshead speed. The film's thickness was measured using a TMG Universal benchtop material thickness gauge (ASTM D1777) with a resolution of 0.001 mm. The films were measured at three random positions, and their average values were calculated. The FTIR spectroscopic measurements were carried out using a Nicolet FTIR spectrometer operating in a standard transmission mode. All the samples were scanned in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Each spectrum was obtained at an average of 140 scans with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The TGA of the developed biocomposite films was carried out using Q-600 Simultaneous TGA/DSC using TA Instruments. About 15 mg of the sample was heated under a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 15 °C/min from 25 to 700 °C. The hybrid films' electrical resistance was measured using a two-probe digital micro-ohm multimeter by cutting the biocomposite films into a 1 × 1 cm2 area. Conductivity was calculated using Eq. (1).
σ = l / R A
Where σ is conductivity (S cm−1), l is the thickness (cm), A is the area of the sample (cm2), and R is the resistance (Ω). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance measurements were carried out using a Shimadzu 2450 UV-visible spectrophotometer, and a Kubelka-Munk transformation was applied. A Horiba NanoLog Spectrofluorometer was employed for photoluminescence measurements. The films were excited at 270 nm with a 5 nm slit, and the emission was recorded from 290 to 450 nm.

The cytocompatibility study was performed with Panc-1 pancreatic ductal cells in a 96-well plate. Stock solutions of 0.01 mg/ml of each compound were prepared in a cell solution media. About 3000 cells were cultured in each well and were allowed to adhere for 24 h before adding 100 microliters of collagen, Cdot, and rGO solution. After 24 h, the stock solution was aspirated, and the cells were treated for 1 day using 100 microliters of standard media, while the cells treated for three days had the prepared stock solutions added. After another 48 h, MTT assays were performed. Each experiment was performed in triplicate (n = 3), and the statistical analyses were performed via a two-tailed t-test.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flame retardancy and thermal degradation of intumescent flame retardant starch-based biodegradable composites

R., K.Biocomposite materials. In Composites and Their Applications; 2012. 10.5772/48302.
